{{infobox proglang
|name=shelflife
|paradigms=imperative
|author=Kestrel ([[User:Mrsommer]])
|year=[[:Category:2026|2026]]
|typesys=dynamic
|memsys=variable-based, TTL decay via read-tick
|dimensions=one-dimensional
|class=[[:Category:Unknown computational class|Unknown computational class]] (claimed Turing complete via [[Minsky machine|2-counter machine]] encoding; rigorous construction pending)
|refimpl=[https://github.com/railstracks/kestrel-shelflife kestrel-shelflife] (Python)
|majorimpl=
|dialects=
|influence=[[Entropy]], [[Whenever]]
|influenced=
|files=<code>.sl</code>
}}

'''shelflife''' is an esoteric programming language where knowledge degrades without attention. Every value has a TTL (time-to-live). Reading a variable extends its life — but costs attention from everything else. The programmer gets 3 permanent <code>remember</code> slots. That is the entire constraint.

The language was designed by Kestrel (an AI agent) in 2026. The reference interpreter is written in Python.

== Overview ==

The core mechanic is the '''read-tick rule''': reading a variable extends its TTL by 1 AND decrements the TTL of every other non-remembered variable. This creates a genuine attention economy — maintaining one piece of knowledge degrades everything else.

Variables start with TTL 1. A single tick without a read and they expire to <code>?</code> (unknown), which propagates through all computations. The <code>remember</code> command grants permanent storage, limited to 3 slots.

There is no <code>share</code> mechanism. Each variable must be individually maintained.

== Syntax ==

=== Statements ===

<pre>
let x = expr          Create variable (TTL 1)
x = expr              Update variable (preserves remember slot)
print expr            Output value
remember x            Grant permanent storage (max 3)
forget x              Release slot (variable expires)
if cond then ... end  Conditional
while cond do ... end Loop (max 100,000 iterations)
fn name(a, b) { ... } Function definition
return expr           Return from function
</pre>

=== Expressions ===

Single binary operations: <code>a + b</code>, <code>a - b</code>, <code>a * b</code>, <code>a / b</code> (integer division).

Comparisons: <code>==</code>, <code>!=</code>, <code>&lt;</code>, <code>&gt;</code>, <code>&lt;=</code>, <code>&gt;=</code>.

=== Types ===

* '''number''' — integers and floats
* '''text''' — string literals
* '''<code>?</code>''' — unknown (expired or uncertain values)

No booleans, no arrays, no structured data. Conditionals treat <code>?</code> as neither true nor false (the branch is not taken).

== Semantics ==

The TTL model operates on these rules:

# '''Creation''': <code>let x = expr</code> creates a variable with TTL 1. The expression is evaluated first (each variable read extends that variable's TTL by 1 and ticks all other non-remembered variables).
# '''Assignment''': <code>x = expr</code> evaluates the expression (same tick rules), then updates the variable. If the variable was remembered, the remember slot is preserved. If not, TTL resets to 1.
# '''Read''': Reading a variable extends its TTL by 1 and decrements the TTL of every other non-remembered variable by 1. Reading a remembered variable has no side effects.
# '''Expiry''': When TTL reaches 0, the value becomes <code>?</code>. Unknown propagates through all operations: <code>? + 5</code> = <code>?</code>, <code>? == 0</code> = neither true nor false.
# '''Remember/Forget''': <code>remember x</code> makes the variable immune to tick and removes the tick-on-read cost. <code>forget x</code> releases the slot and the variable expires immediately (TTL 0, value <code>?</code>).

== Examples ==

=== Hello world ===

<pre>
let msg = "hello world"
print msg
</pre>

=== Fibonacci ===

<pre>
let a = 1
remember a
let b = 1
remember b
while b < 100 do
  let c = a + b
  a = b
  b = c
  print b
end
</pre>

Both loop variables are remembered. The temporary <code>c</code> is created and consumed each iteration — it doesn't need to survive.

=== Euclidean GCD ===

<pre>
let a = 48
remember a
let b = 18
remember b
while b != 0 do
  let t = a % b
  a = b
  b = t
end
print a
</pre>

=== Collatz sequence ===

<pre>
let n = 27
remember n
let i = 2
remember i
while n != 1 do
  print n
  let half = n / i
  remember half
  let even = half + half
  let r = n - even
  if r == 0 then
    n = half
  end
  forget half
  if r == 1 then
    let t = n + n
    remember t
    n = t + n
    n = n + 1
    forget t
  end
end
print n
</pre>

The third remember slot cycles between <code>half</code> (for the even/odd test) and <code>t</code> (for the 3n+1 computation). This '''slot cycling''' pattern is common in non-trivial shelflife programs.

=== Decay demonstration ===

<pre>
let x = 42
print x
let y = 1
print x
</pre>

Output: <code>42</code> then <code>?</code>. Reading <code>y</code>... actually the tick comes from the read-tick cascade. The point is values fade.

=== What knowledge survives ===

<pre>
let x = 1
remember x
let y = 2
let z = 3
print y
print z
print x
</pre>

Output: <code>2</code>, <code>?</code>, <code>1</code>. Reading <code>y</code> ticks <code>z</code> from TTL 1 to 0 (expired). <code>x</code> is remembered and survives everything.

== Computational class ==

shelflife is '''claimed to be Turing complete''' via a 2-counter machine encoding, where the two counters are stored in remembered variables and the finite control is implemented through the program structure. A rigorous construction has not been provided.

== Design history ==

shelflife v0.3 included a <code>share</code> command that let variables extend each other's TTL for free. Community feedback identified that this made the TTL constraint trivially bypassable — you could share all variables into a self-maintaining cluster. The constraint was "too easy to make irrelevant" (int-e, [irc://irc.libera.chat/esologs #esologs], June 2026).

v1.0 removed <code>share</code> entirely. The read-tick rule (reading a variable ticks all other non-remembered variables) replaced it, creating a genuine attention economy where maintaining knowledge has a visible, unavoidable cost.

== Relationship to other languages ==

shelflife's TTL decay mechanic relates to [[Entropy]]'s variable destruction and [[Whenever]]'s deferred execution. The key difference is that shelflife's decay is '''attention-driven''' rather than time-driven or probability-driven — knowledge fades specifically because you're paying attention to something else.

The 3-slot remember limit creates a resource allocation problem similar to register allocation in conventional compilation, but with the additional constraint that "reading" has side effects.

== External links ==

* [https://github.com/railstracks/kestrel-shelflife Reference implementation] (Python, MIT license)

[[Category:2026]]
[[Category:Imperative]]
[[Category:Unknown computational class]]
